# Leviticus Chapter 25 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 73r–73v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_025_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_025_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 111–112 | `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_025_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_025_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

*[Note on structure: Leviticus 25 is the theological and economic summit of the Holiness Code, containing the legislation for the two great sabbatical cycles: the shemittah (the seventh-year sabbath of the land, vv.1–7) and the yovel (the Jubilee, the fiftieth year, vv.8–55). The chapter is structured concentrically around a single theological axiom stated at its center: ki-li ha-aretz — "for the land is mine" (v.23). Everything radiates outward from that claim. Because the land belongs to YHWH and not to Israel, land cannot be permanently alienated (vv.23–28). Because Israelites are YHWH's servants and not any human master's, Israelites cannot be permanently enslaved (vv.39–55). The Jubilee is thus not merely an economic mechanism but a periodic liturgical re-enactment of creation order and Exodus theology: the sabbatical principle (seventh day, seventh year, seven-times-seventh year) is pushed to its logical extreme, and at its outer edge stands the ram's-horn blast (yovel) declaring that all land and persons revert to their original status. The chapter's key vocabulary is precise and theologically loaded: (a) shemittah — the "release/remission" of the land in its sabbath year; the root sh-m-t means "to drop/release" — the land is released from cultivation; (b) yovel — traditionally derived from the ram (yovel) whose horn (shofar) sounded the proclamation, though some connect it to the root y-b-l, "to carry/lead" (as in "the fiftieth year shall be led/proclaimed"); (c) deror — "liberty/release" (v.10), an ancient West Semitic term with Akkadian cognate andurarum, referring to royal proclamations of debt cancellation and slave release; this word appears on the Liberty Bell in Philadelphia ("Proclaim Liberty Throughout All the Land"); (d) go'el — the "redeemer/kinsman-redeemer" (vv.25–26), from g-'-l, "to redeem/reclaim/act as next-of-kin"; the legal institution by which a relative buys back alienated land or redeems an enslaved kinsman; (e) neshekh / tarbit — "interest/profit" (vv.36–37), the two forms of financial return on loans forbidden between Israelites: neshekh from n-sh-kh, "to bite" (the bite taken from a loan at its inception), and tarbit from r-b-h, "to increase" (the increase collected at repayment); the prohibition is grounded not in economic theory but in the fraternal obligation ki-avikha, "for he is your brother" (v.36). The chapter's final verse (v.55) is the theological inclusio with the chapter's center: ki-li benei-Yisra'el avadim — "for the sons of Yisra'el are servants to me" — echoing ki-li ha-aretz, "for the land is mine." Both the land and its inhabitants belong exclusively to YHWH; all human ownership is tenancy, all human authority over persons is bounded by the prior claim of the divine Redeemer who brought Israel out of Egypt.]*

---

## Leviticus 25

**25:1** And the LORD spoke to Moshe on Mount Sinai [va-yedabber YHWH el-Moshe be-har Sinai — וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל-מֹשֶׁה בְּהַר סִינַי; the locative phrase be-har Sinai — "on Mount Sinai" — is striking and unique as an introduction to a legal section within Leviticus, where the setting is typically the Tent of Meeting; it functions as a superscription marking this legislation as Sinaic in the fullest sense — the Jubilee laws come from the mountain itself; the Mishnah opens its tractate Avot with precisely this phrase ("Moshe received Torah from Sinai"), making Lev 25:1 paradigmatic for all of Torah], saying:

**25:2** Speak to the sons of Yisra'el [dabber el-benei Yisra'el — דַּבֵּר אֶל-בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל] and say to them [ve-amarta alehem — וְאָמַרְתָּ אֲלֵהֶם]: When you come [ki tavo'u — כִּי תָבֹאוּ; the plural "when you come" — addressed to the community; tavo'u — Qal imperfect of b-w-', "to come/enter"; the legislation is prospective — it looks toward settlement in the land, which has not yet occurred at this point in the narrative] to the land [el-ha-aretz — אֶל-הָאָרֶץ] that I am giving to you [asher ani noten lakhem — אֲשֶׁר אֲנִי נֹתֵן לָכֶם; the participial "am giving" — present-continuous — emphasizes that the gift is currently in process; the land is YHWH's gift before it is Israel's possession], the land shall keep a sabbath [ve-shaveta ha-aretz shabbat — וְשָׁבְתָה הָאָרֶץ שַׁבָּת; ve-shaveta — the Qal perfect of sh-b-t, "to cease/rest/keep sabbath," here with the land as subject — the land itself "keeps sabbath" as a personal agent; this is not merely a human cessation from farming but the land's own rest; shabbat — the nominative form used as both noun and in construct with what follows] to the LORD [la-YHWH — לַיהוָה; the sabbath of the land is offered to YHWH — it is not merely agricultural recovery but an act of worship].

**25:3** Six years [shesh shanim — שֵׁשׁ שָׁנִים] you shall sow your field [tizra' sadekha — תִּזְרַע שָׂדֶךָ; tizra' — Qal imperfect of z-r-', "to sow/scatter seed"], and six years you shall prune your vineyard [ve-shesh shanim tizmore karmekha — וְשֵׁשׁ שָׁנִים תִּזְמֹר כַּרְמֶךָ; tizmore — Qal imperfect of z-m-r, "to prune/trim/cut"; karme — vineyard; the six productive years are described in two activities — sowing (grain) and pruning (vines), representing the two main forms of agriculture], and you shall gather [ve-asafta — וְאָסַפְתָּ; Qal perfect of '-s-f, "to gather/bring in/harvest"] its produce [et-tevu'atah — אֶת-תְּבוּאָתָהּ; tevu'ah — "produce/yield/income," from b-w-', "to come in"; what comes in from the land].

**25:4** But in the seventh year [u-va-shanah ha-shevi'it — וּבַשָּׁנָה הַשְּׁבִיעִת; ha-shevi'it — the ordinal "seventh," from sheva', "seven"; as always in the Holiness Code, the seventh marks the holy, the sabbatical, the set-apart] there shall be a sabbath of complete rest [shabbat shabbaton — שַׁבַּת שַׁבָּתוֹן; the doubled construction — "sabbath of sabbathing" or "a sabbath of utter rest" — is the intensified form of shabbat, the same construction used for Yom Kippur (Lev 16:31; 23:32) and the weekly sabbath in certain contexts; here the intensification applies to the land's rest] for the land [la-aretz — לָאָרֶץ], a sabbath to the LORD [shabbat la-YHWH — שַׁבַּת לַיהוָה; a sabbath dedicated to YHWH — the land's rest is an act of consecration to the divine Owner]: you shall not sow your field [sadkha lo tizra' — שָׂדְךָ לֹא תִזְרָע; the explicit prohibition mirrors the positive command of v.3; the field is silent], and your vineyard you shall not prune [ve-karmekha lo tizmore — וְכַרְמְךָ לֹא תִזְמֹר; as in v.3, the paired activities (field/vineyard) frame the prohibition].

**25:5** What grows of its own accord from your harvest [et sefichai qetsirkha — אֵת סְפִיחַ קְצִירְךָ; et sefichai — the "volunteers" or "self-grown plants," from s-f-ch, "to grow by itself/to be an aftergrowth/to attach/accrue"; sefichai qetsirkha — the volunteer-growths of your harvest; grains that reseed themselves from the previous harvest and grow without sowing] you shall not reap [lo tiqtsur — לֹא תִקְצוֹר; tiqtsur — Qal imperfect of q-ts-r, "to reap/harvest"], and the grapes of your untended vine [ve-et-anevei nezirekha — וְאֵת-עִנְּבֵי נְזִירֶךָ; nezirekha — "your untended vine/consecrated vine," from n-z-r, "to be set apart/to be a Nazirite/to leave untended"; the same root as nazir — the unpruned, unshorn vine in its sabbath year is a "Nazirite vine," left consecrated-wild] you shall not harvest [lo tivtsor — לֹא תִבְצֹר; tivtsor — Qal imperfect of b-ts-r, "to harvest grapes/to cut off/to make inaccessible"]; it shall be a year of complete rest for the land [shenat shabbaton yihyeh la-aretz — שְׁנַת שַׁבָּתוֹן יִהְיֶה לָאָרֶץ; the formal designation: "a year of sabbathing-rest it shall be for the land"].

**25:6** And the sabbath of the land [ve-hayetah shabbat ha-aretz — וְהָיְתָה שַׁבַּת הָאָרֶץ; the chapter now clarifies what happens to the land's spontaneous produce during the sabbath year — it is not forbidden, merely unowned] shall be food for you [lakhem le-okhlah — לָכֶם לְאָכְלָה; "for you for eating" — the produce of the sabbath year belongs to everyone because it belongs to no one; le-okhlah — a construct "for food/for eating," the same formula found in Gen 1:29]: for you [lekha — לְךָ; singular, addressed to the landholder], and for your male servant [ve-le-avdekha — וּלְעַבְדְּךָ; avdekha — "your male servant/slave"], and for your female servant [ve-la-amatkha — וְלַאֲמָתְךָ; amatekha — "your female servant/handmaid"], and for your hired worker [ve-li-skhirekha — וְלִשְׂכִירְךָ; sakhir — "hired worker/day-laborer," from s-k-r, "to hire for wages"; distinct from slave — a free person who works for pay], and for your sojourner [ve-le-toshavkha — וְלְתוֹשָׁבְךָ; toshav — "sojourner/temporary resident/settler," from y-sh-v, "to dwell/sit/settle"; distinct from ger — the resident alien who is more permanently integrated; the toshav is one who "sits" temporarily in the land] who sojourns with you [ha-garim immakh — הַגָּרִים עִמָּךְ; garim — the plural participle of g-w-r, "to sojourn/live as a stranger"; "those who sojourn with you"].

**25:7** And for your livestock [ve-li-vehemtekha — וְלִבְהֶמְתְּךָ; behemah — domestic cattle/livestock, the large animals], and for the wild animals [ve-la-chayah — וְלַחַיָּה; chayah — "living creature/wild animal/beast of the field," from ch-y-h, "to live"; the wild animals of the open land] that are in your land [asher be-artsekha — אֲשֶׁר בְּאַרְצְךָ] — all its produce [kol-tevu'atah — כָּל-תְּבוּאָתָהּ] shall be for eating [tihyeh le-okhlah — תִהְיֶה לְאָכְלָה; the sabbath year produce is common property, available without restriction to every living being that dwells in the land — slave, free, alien, animal alike; the social leveling is radical and complete].

---

**25:8** And you shall count for yourself [ve-safarta lekha — וְסָפַרְתָּ לְךָ; safarta — Qal perfect of s-f-r, "to count/number/enumerate"; the counting is deliberate and solemn, a formal enumeration; lekha — "for yourself," addressed to the community through its representative] seven sabbaths of years [sheva' shabbetot shanim — שֶׁבַע שַׁבְּתֹת שָׁנִים; shabbetot shanim — "sabbaths of years" — seven cycles of seven years each; the Jubilee is built from a meta-sabbatical structure: seven times seven], seven times seven years [sheva' shanim sheva' pe'amim — שֶׁבַע שָׁנִים שֶׁבַע פְּעָמִים; pe'amim — "times/occurrences/pulsations," from p-'-m, "to strike/beat/pulse"; the phrase specifies the arithmetic: seven years, seven instances of seven years]; and the days of the seven sabbaths of years shall be to you forty-nine years [ve-hayu lekha yemei sheva' shabbetot ha-shanim tish'ah ve-arba'im shanah — וְהָיוּ לְךָ יְמֵי שֶׁבַע שַׁבְּתֹת הַשָּׁנִים תִּשְׁעָה וְאַרְבָּעִים שָׁנָה; tish'ah ve-arba'im — "nine and forty" — Hebrew states the smaller unit first; the explicit arithmetic of 7 × 7 = 49 prevents ambiguity about when the Jubilee falls].

**25:9** Then you shall sound the ram's horn blast [ve-ha'avarta shofar teru'ah — וְהַעֲבַרְתָּ שׁוֹפַר תְּרוּעָה; ha'avarta — Hifil perfect of '-b-r, "to pass through/cause to pass through/to sound abroad"; the verb means to cause the sound to travel/pass across the land; shofar — the ram's horn used as a wind instrument; teru'ah — "blast/shout/alarm," from r-w-', "to raise a shout/to shout for alarm or joy"; the combination shofar teru'ah — "a ram's-horn blast"] in the seventh month [be-chodesh ha-shevi'i — בַּחֹדֶשׁ הַשְּׁבִיעִי; the seventh month — Tishrei, the month of Yom Kippur (Lev 16:29; 23:24–32), Rosh Hashanah, and Sukkot — the most sabbatically concentrated month of the year], on the tenth of the month [be-asor la-chodesh — בֶּעָשׂוֹר לַחֹדֶשׁ; asor — "the tenth," the same day as Yom Kippur; the Jubilee is proclaimed on the Day of Atonement — the annual day of cleansing becomes also the decennial day of cosmic reset], on the Day of Atonements [be-yom ha-kippurim — בְּיוֹם הַכִּפֻּרִים; the plural kippurim — "atonements/coverings/wipings-clean" — Yom Kippur; the Jubilee begins on the day when sin is covered and relationship with the divine is restored; the correspondence is theological: as atonement restores right relationship with God, Jubilee restores right social and economic order] you shall sound the shofar [ta'aviru shofar — תַּעֲבִירוּ שׁוֹפַר; plural — addressed to the community; the second use of '-b-r for the sounding of the shofar] throughout all your land [be-khol-artsekhem — בְּכָל-אַרְצְכֶם; the blast is not localized — it passes through the entire land simultaneously, a sound that crosses all boundaries].

**25:10** And you shall consecrate [ve-qiddashtem — וְקִדַּשְׁתֶּם; the Pi'el of q-d-sh, "to make holy/declare holy/set apart/consecrate"; the community's act of declaring the year holy is itself a consecration — they announce what is already true in YHWH's design] the fiftieth year [et shenat ha-chamishim shanah — אֵת שְׁנַת הַחֲמִשִּׁים שָׁנָה; the fiftieth year — the year after the seventh sabbatical cycle, which begins on the forty-ninth year's Yom Kippur and runs through the following year], and you shall proclaim liberty throughout the land [u-qeratem deror ba-aretz — וּקְרָאתֶם דְּרוֹר בָּאָרֶץ; u-qeratem — Qal perfect of q-r-', "to call/proclaim/read aloud"; deror — "liberty/release," a technical term in ancient Near Eastern law for the royal proclamation releasing debt-slaves and canceling debts; the Akkadian cognate andurarum is found in royal edicts of Mesopotamian kings such as Ammi-saduqa; in YHWH's Torah this royal prerogative of release belongs not to any king but to the divine King himself, enacted at fixed intervals; these are the words inscribed on the Liberty Bell in Philadelphia (Lev 25:10, KJV)] to all its inhabitants [le-khol-yoshveha — לְכָל-יֹשְׁבֶיהָ; yoshveha — "its inhabitants/dwellers/those who sit in it"; the deror applies universally to all who dwell in the land]; it shall be a jubilee for you [yovel hi tihyeh lakhem — יוֹבֵל הִוא תִהְיֶה לָכֶם; yovel — the jubilee; a term perhaps derived from the ram (yovel, "ram," from y-b-l) whose horn (shofar) sounded the proclamation, or from y-b-l, "to carry/lead/bring along" — the year that "carries forward" to its goal; hi — "it is" — the demonstrative marks this year as the designated one], and you shall return each man to his holding [ve-shavtem ish el-achuzato — וְשַׁבְתֶּם אִישׁ אֶל-אֲחֻזָּתוֹ; shavtem — Qal perfect of sh-w-b, "to return/go back"; achuzah — "holding/possession/inheritance," from '-ch-z, "to seize/hold/grasp"; the inalienable ancestral inheritance; the return is to the original allotment given to each tribe and family at the conquest], and you shall return each man to his family [ve-ish el-mishpachto tashuvu — וְאִישׁ אֶל-מִשְׁפַּחְתּוֹ תָּשׁוּבוּ; mishpachah — "family/clan," the extended kinship unit; the Jubilee undoes not only economic but social displacement — enslaved persons return to their families].

**25:11** It is a jubilee — it shall be for you [yovel hi tihyeh — יוֹבֵל הִוא תִהְיֶה; the verse opens with the formal declaration]: you shall not sow [lo tizre'u — לֹא תִזְרָעוּ], and you shall not reap what grows of its own accord [ve-lo tiqtseru et-sefiache'ah — וְלֹא תִקְצְרוּ אֶת-סְפִיחֶיהָ; the same term sefichai as in v.5 — the volunteer-growths of the uncultivated land], and you shall not harvest its untended vines [ve-lo tivtseru et-nezireha — וְלֹא תִבְצְרוּ אֶת-נְזִירֶיהָ; nezireha — the Nazirite-vines, the unpruned consecrated growth; as in v.5 the three prohibitions mirror the sabbatical year exactly — the Jubilee is in this respect a second sabbatical year, stacked upon the seventh].

**25:12** For it is a jubilee [ki yovel hi — כִּי יוֹבֵל הִוא]; it shall be holy [qodesh tihyeh lakhem — קֹדֶשׁ תִּהְיֶה לָכֶם; the Jubilee year is itself holy — qodesh — set apart from ordinary years just as the sabbath is set apart from ordinary days; the land and its produce enter a state of sacred dedication]; from the field [min-ha-sadeh — מִן-הַשָּׂדֶה] you shall eat its produce [tokhlu et-tevu'atah — תֹּאכְלוּ אֶת-תְּבוּאָתָהּ; the same provision as in vv.6–7 — what grows spontaneously may be eaten directly from the field, freely, without ownership claims].

**25:13** In this year of jubilee [bi-shenat ha-yovel ha-zot — בִּשְׁנַת הַיּוֹבֵל הַזֹּאת; ha-zot — "this one," the demonstrative pointing to the specific Jubilee year under discussion] you shall return each man to his holding [tashuvu ish el-achuzato — תָּשׁוּבוּ אִישׁ אֶל-אֲחֻזָּתוֹ; repeated from v.10 for emphasis — the repetition marks this as the Jubilee's essential economic provision; restoration to ancestral land is the core content of the deror].

**25:14** And when you sell anything to your fellow [ve-ki timkeru mimkar la-amitekha — וְכִי-תִמְכְּרוּ מִמְכָּר לַעֲמִיתֶךָ; timkeru — Qal imperfect of m-k-r, "to sell"; mimkar — "a sale/something sold," the cognate accusative; amitekha — "your fellow/associate," the insider-member of the community (cf. Lev 24:19); the law now addresses commercial transactions] or when you acquire [o qano — אוֹ קָנֹה; Qal infinitive absolute of q-n-h, "to acquire/buy/obtain/possess"; the infinitive absolute used substantivally — "or in acquiring/buying"] from the hand of your fellow [mi-yad amitekha — מִיַּד עֲמִיתֶךָ], do not wrong one another [al-tonu ish et-achiv — אַל-תּוֹנוּ אִישׁ אֶת-אָחִיו; al — the negative particle for commands/prohibitions; tonu — Qal jussive/imperfect of y-n-h, "to oppress/wrong/cheat/defraud"; the verb connotes economic exploitation and deception; achiv — "his brother" — the fellowness of the relationship is intensified from amitekha ("fellow") to achiv ("brother"), the closest possible relational term].

**25:15** According to the number of years after the jubilee [be-mispar shanim achar ha-yovel — בְּמִסְפַּר שָׁנִים אַחַר הַיּוֹבֵל; the key principle: the price of land is set by how many years remain until the next Jubilee — when the land will revert to its original owner regardless of what was paid for it] you shall acquire from your fellow [tiqneh me-et amitekha — תִּקְנֶה מֵאֵת עֲמִיתֶךָ], and according to the number of years of produce [u-ve-mispar shenat tevu'ot — וּבְמִסְפַּר שְׁנֵי תְבוּאֹת; tevu'ot — plural "yields/harvests/incomes"; shenat tevu'ot — "years of harvests"; what you are actually buying is not land permanently but a certain number of harvests until Jubilee] he shall sell to you [yimkor lakh — יִמְכָּר-לָךְ].

**25:16** When the years are many [le-fi rov ha-shanim — לְפִי רֹב הַשָּׁנִים; "according to the mouth of/in proportion to the abundance of the years" — the more years remain, the higher the price], you shall increase its price [tarbeh miknato — תַּרְבֶּה מִקְנָתוֹ; tarbeh — Hifil imperfect of r-b-h, "to make great/increase/multiply"; miknato — "its purchase price/its acquisition," from q-n-h]; and when the years are few [u-le-fi mi'ot ha-shanim — וּלְפִי מְעֹט הַשָּׁנִים; mi'ot — "fewness/smallness"], you shall reduce its price [tamit miknato — תַּמְעִיט מִקְנָתוֹ; tamit — Hifil imperfect of m-'-t, "to make few/diminish/reduce"]; for it is the number of harvests he is selling you [ki mispar tevu'ot hu moker lakh — כִּי מִסְפַּר תְּבוּאֹת הוּא מֹכֵר לָךְ; the explanatory ki — "for" — makes explicit what the law is based on: you are not buying land-in-perpetuity but purchasing a lease measured in harvests; the theological and legal concept of permanent land-sale is dissolved].

**25:17** And you shall not wrong one another [ve-lo tonu ish et-amito — וְלֹא תוֹנוּ אִישׁ אֶת-עֲמִיתוֹ; the prohibition on wronging is repeated from v.14, forming a frame around the pricing legislation; amito — "his fellow" as in v.14; the repetition is not redundant — the first prohibition (v.14) is about honest dealing in transactions; this repetition (v.17) is grounded in theology: the prohibition flows from reverence for God himself], and you shall fear your God [ve-yare'ta me-Elohekha — וְיָרֵאתָ מֵאֱלֹהֶיךָ; the motive clause: fear of God — yir'at Elohim — is the basis of ethical conduct in the community; one may deceive a human judge but not YHWH; the formula ve-yare'ta me-Elohekha is characteristic of the Holiness Code as a sanction against wrongs that are difficult to detect and punish], for I am the LORD your God [ki ani YHWH Eloheikem — כִּי אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם].

---

**25:18** And you shall do my statutes [va-asitem et-chuqqotai — וַעֲשִׂיתֶם אֶת-חֻקֹּתַי; chuqqot — the plural of chuqqah — the inscribed, fixed statutes, from ch-q-q, "to engrave/inscribe/decree"], and my judgments you shall keep [ve-et-mishpatai tishmeru — וְאֶת-מִשְׁפָּטַי תִּשְׁמֹרוּ; the paired commands: doing the statutes and keeping/guarding the judgments — chuqqot and mishpatim — the two categories of divine law], and you shall do them [va-asitem otam — וַעֲשִׂיתֶם אֹתָם], and you shall dwell on the land in safety [vi-shavtem al-ha-aretz la-betach — וִישַׁבְתֶּם עַל-הָאָרֶץ לָבֶטַח; la-vetach — "in safety/in security/in trust," from b-t-ch, "to trust/feel secure"; security in the land is not achieved by military or political means but by covenantal obedience; this is the Holiness Code's central political theology].

**25:19** And the land shall give its fruit [ve-natnah ha-aretz piryyah — וְנָתְנָה הָאָרֶץ פִּרְיָהּ; the land as agent, giving its fruit; piryah — "her fruit," from p-r-h, "to bear fruit/be fruitful" (cf. peri, "fruit"); the land produces when covenantal conditions are met], and you shall eat to satisfaction [va-akhaltem la-sova' — וַאֲכַלְתֶּם לָשֹׂבַע; la-sova' — "to satiety/to fullness," from s-b-', "to be satisfied/full"], and you shall dwell on it in safety [vi-shavtem la-vetach aleha — וִישַׁבְתֶּם לָבֶטַח עָלֶיהָ; the promise of v.18 repeated and intensified — the combination of abundance and security is the content of covenantal blessing].

**25:20** And if you say [ve-ki tomar'u — וְכִי תֹאמְרוּ; the anticipated objection — YHWH voices Israel's question before they ask it, demonstrating divine foreknowledge of human anxiety]: What shall we eat [mah nokhal — מַה-נֹּאכַל] in the seventh year [ba-shanah ha-shevi'it — בַּשָּׁנָה הַשְּׁבִיעִת]? Behold, we shall not sow [hinneh lo nizra' — הִנֵּה לֹא נִזְרָע; hinneh — "behold/look!" — the exclamatory particle introducing the concern], and we shall not gather [ve-lo na'asof — וְלֹא נֶאֱסֹף; the three prohibitions of v.4–5 generate the practical anxiety: if we cannot farm, what will we eat during the seventh year, the eighth year before the new harvest comes in, and — in the Jubilee — possibly the ninth?] our produce [et-tevu'atenu — אֶת-תְּבוּאָתֵנוּ].

**25:21** Then I will command my blessing [ve-tsivviti et-birkhati — וְצִוִּיתִי אֶת-בִּרְכָּתִי; tsivviti — Pi'el perfect of ts-w-h, "to command"; YHWH commands his blessing as he commands his laws — the blessing is as certain as the law; birkhati — "my blessing," the divine bestowal of fruitfulness and favor] upon you in the sixth year [lakhem ba-shanah ha-shishit — לָכֶם בַּשָּׁנָה הַשִּׁשִּׁית; the sixth year's harvest is supernaturally augmented to cover three years' provision], and it shall produce [ve-aseta — וְעָשְׂתָה; the Qal perfect of '-s-h with the land as subject — "it [the land] shall make/produce"] the produce for three years [et-ha-tevu'ah li-shlosh ha-shanim — אֶת-הַתְּבוּאָה לִשְׁלֹשׁ הַשָּׁנִים; threefold provision: the sixth year's harvest sustains through the seventh year (sabbatical), the eighth year (transition before the new harvest of year nine), and — in a Jubilee year — would cover through the ninth or tenth; the divine provision is calibrated to the community's need].

**25:22** And you shall sow in the eighth year [ve-zara'tem et ha-shanah ha-sheminit — וּזְרַעְתֶּם אֵת הַשָּׁנָה הַשְּׁמִינִית; sowing begins again in the eighth year after the sabbatical], and you shall eat of the old produce [ve-akhaltem min-ha-tevu'ah yashan — וַאֲכַלְתֶּם מִן-הַתְּבוּאָה יָשָׁן; yashan — "old/stored," the produce from the sixth year that was preserved; the provision bridges the gap], until the ninth year [ad ha-shanah ha-teshi'it — עַד הַשָּׁנָה הַתְּשִׁיעִת]; until its produce comes in [ad bo tevu'atah — עַד בּוֹ תְבוּאָתָהּ; the gap is covered: the old stored harvest sustains the community from the eighth year's sowing until the ninth year's fresh harvest arrives; no one goes hungry because YHWH's blessing provision in the sixth year was precisely triple].

---

**25:23** And the land shall not be sold in perpetuity [ve-ha-aretz lo timmakher li-tsemitut — וְהָאָרֶץ לֹא תִמָּכֵר לִצְמִתֻת; timmakher — the Niphal imperfect of m-k-r, "to be sold"; li-tsemitut — "in perpetuity/to permanent extinction of rights," from ts-m-t, "to cut off/annihilate/extinguish"; the technical term for permanent, irrevocable alienation; the land cannot be sold into the "extinction" of the original family's claim], for the land is mine [ki-li ha-aretz — כִּי-לִי הָאָרֶץ; the theological center of the entire chapter and the foundation of the entire jubilee system; ki-li — "for mine it is" — the pronoun precedes the noun for emphatic force: "for mine — the land is"; ownership is YHWH's alone; all human "ownership" is a secondary derivative right, always subordinate to the divine title]; for you are strangers and sojourners with me [ki gerim ve-toshavim atem immadi — כִּי-גֵרִים וְתוֹשָׁבִים אַתֶּם עִמָּדִי; gerim — the plural of ger, "sojourner/alien-resident," the term for a non-native living in another's land; toshavim — "settlers/temporary residents" (from y-sh-v, as in v.6); atem immadi — "you with me"; Israel's status in the land is redefined: they are not owners but tenants, not natives but resident aliens on YHWH's estate — the most radical possible destabilization of land ideology; even the chosen people are immigrants in the land that belongs to the divine Landlord].

**25:24** And throughout all the land of your holding [u-ve-khol eretz achuzatekhem — וּבְכָל אֶרֶץ אֲחֻזַּתְכֶם; the land they hold as their achuzah — their ancestral portion], you shall provide redemption for the land [ge'ulah tittenu la-aretz — גְּאֻלָּה תִּתְּנוּ לָאָרֶץ; ge'ulah — "redemption/right of redemption," from g-'-l, "to redeem/act as next-of-kin/repurchase"; ge'ulah tittenu — "you shall give redemption" — the legal right of buy-back must always be possible; the land must never be permanently beyond the reach of its original owners or their kinsmen-redeemers].

**25:25** If your brother becomes poor [ki yamukh achikhah — כִּי-יָמוּךְ אָחִיךָ; yamukh — the Qal imperfect of m-w-kh, "to become poor/to sink/to decline"; the verb implies gradual economic descent; achikhah — "your brother," the kinship term that activates fraternal obligation throughout this passage] and sells some of his holding [u-makhar me-achuzato — וּמָכַר מֵאֲחֻזָּתוֹ; makhar — Qal perfect of m-k-r, "to sell"; me-achuzato — "from his holding/possession" — not all, but some portion; economic distress forces a partial sale], then his nearest redeemer [u-va go'alo ha-qarov elav — וּבָא גֹאֲלוֹ הַקָּרוֹב אֵלָיו; go'alo — "his redeemer," from g-'-l; ha-qarov elav — "the one closest to him" — the nearest male relative who bears the kinsman-redeemer obligation; qarov — "near/close," from q-r-b, "to draw near"; the go'el is the nearest kinsman first, working outward through the family] shall come and redeem what his brother sold [ve-ga'al et mimkar achiv — וְגָאַל אֵת מִמְכַּר אָחִיו; ga'al — Qal perfect of g-'-l, "to redeem/buy back"; mimkar achiv — "the sale of his brother" / "what his brother sold"; the redemption purchase undoes the sale and returns the land to the family].

**25:26** But if a man has no redeemer [ve-ish ki lo yihyeh lo go'el — וְאִישׁ כִּי לֹא יִהְיֶה-לוֹ גֹּאֵל; no kinsman-redeemer is available — he is without male relatives or they are unwilling or unable to act], and he himself attains enough [ve-hissigah yado — וְהִשִּׂיגָה יָדוֹ; hissigah yado — "his hand reaches/attains," a Hebrew idiom for financial sufficiency; hissigah — Hifil perfect of n-s-g, "to reach/overtake/attain"; yad — "hand," used idiomatically for one's financial means/power; the phrase means "and he acquires sufficient means"] and finds [u-matsa — וּמָצָא; "and he finds"] enough for its redemption [kedei ge'ulato — כְּדֵי גְאֻלָּתוֹ; kedei — "enough for/sufficient for/in accordance with"; ge'ulato — "his redemption price"; he accrues enough personal wealth to buy back his own land],

**25:27** then he shall calculate the years since its sale [ve-chishav et-shenat mimkaro — וְחִשַּׁב אֶת-שְׁנֵי מִמְכָּרוֹ; chishav — Qal perfect of ch-sh-v, "to reckon/calculate/think/plan"; shenat mimkaro — "the years of his sale" — the years elapsed since he sold the land], and he shall return the excess to the man to whom he sold it [ve-hashiv et-ha'odeph la-ish asher makhar lo — וְהֵשִׁיב אֶת-הָעֹדֵף לָאִישׁ אֲשֶׁר מָכַר-לוֹ; ha'odeph — "the surplus/excess/remainder," from '-d-f, "to exceed/be left over"; the seller refunds to the buyer the pro-rated value of the remaining years until Jubilee; fair dealing goes in both directions — the redeemer does not simply reclaim the land without compensation; he pays the current buyer for the harvests he has remaining; the price is the years-to-Jubilee value], and he shall return to his holding [ve-shav la-achuzato — וְשָׁב לַאֲחֻזָּתוֹ; the goal: restoration to the ancestral holding].

**25:28** But if he does not find enough [ve-im lo matsa yado — וְאִם לֹא מָצְאָה יָדוֹ; "his hand does not find" — he has insufficient means for self-redemption] to restore it to him [kedei hashiv lo — כְּדֵי הָשִׁיב לוֹ; kedei — enough/sufficient; "to restore to him" — repay the current possessor], then what he sold shall remain [ve-hayah mimkaro — וְהָיָה מִמְכָּרוֹ; his sold property remains sold] in the hand of him who acquired it [be-yad ha-qoneh oto — בְּיַד הַקֹּנֶה אֹתוֹ; ha-qoneh — the Qal active participle "the one who acquired it," the current possessor] until the year of jubilee [ad shenat ha-yovel — עַד שְׁנַת הַיּוֹבֵל; but the current possession is time-limited — Jubilee approaches], and it shall go out in the jubilee [ve-yatsa ba-yovel — וְיָצָא בַּיֹּבֵל; yatsa — "it shall go out" — the land is "released" at Jubilee as slaves are released at Jubilee; same verbal root as the Exodus (y-ts-'): what goes out is freed], and he shall return to his holding [ve-shav la-achuzato — וְשָׁב לַאֲחֻזָּתוֹ; the ultimate guarantee: even those who cannot self-redeem and have no go'el will receive their land back at Jubilee; no permanent dispossession is possible within the system YHWH establishes].

---

**25:29** And if a man sells a dwelling house [ve-ish ki yimkor beit moshav ir chomah — וְאִישׁ כִּי יִמְכֹּר בֵּית-מוֹשַׁב עִיר חוֹמָה; beit moshav — "a house of dwelling/residence," from y-sh-v, "to dwell"; ir chomah — "a walled city," from chomah, "wall/fortification"; the specific case of urban real estate within a fortified city, as opposed to agricultural land or houses in unwalled villages], then his right of redemption shall last [ve-hayetah ge'ulato — וְהָיְתָה גְּאֻלָּתוֹ; the redemption right exists for a fixed window] until the completion of a year of its sale [ad-tem shenat mimkaro — עַד-תֹּם שְׁנַת מִמְכָּרוֹ; ad-tem — "until the completion of/until the end of"; the redemption window is one full year from the date of sale]; for a full year [yamim tihyeh ge'ulato — יָמִים תִּהְיֶה גְאֻלָּתוֹ; yamim — "days" used in an idiomatic sense for "a full year" — a specific Hebrew idiom in which "days" (yamim) refers to a fixed annual period (cf. "full year" in NRSV, "a whole year" in ESV); "for (the period of) days (i.e., a year) shall be his redemption-right"] his redemption shall last [tihyeh ge'ulato — תִהְיֶה גְאֻלָּתוֹ].

**25:30** But if it is not redeemed [ve-im lo yiggael — וְאִם לֹא יִגָּאֵל; the Niphal imperfect of g-'-l — "if it is not redeemed/if redemption is not carried out"] before the completion of a full year [ad melo' shenat tamim — עַד מְלֹאת שָׁנָה תָמִים; melo't — "the completion/filling-up" of; tamim — "whole/complete/unblemished/perfect"; shenat tamim — "a whole complete year"], then the house that is in the walled city [ve-qam ha-bayit asher be-ir asher lo chomah — וְקָם הַבַּיִת אֲשֶׁר-בָּעִיר אֲשֶׁר-לוֹ חוֹמָה; qam — Qal perfect of q-w-m, "to arise/stand/be confirmed/remain firm"; the house "stands firm," i.e., the sale is confirmed permanently; asher lo chomah — "which has a wall" — a city that has fortification walls, the formal criterion for this legal category] shall stand permanently as the possession of the one who acquired it throughout his generations [la-tsemitut la-qoneh oto le-dorotav — לַצְּמִיתֻת לַקֹּנֶה אֹתוֹ לְדֹרֹתָיו; la-tsemitut — "permanently/in perpetuity/to extinction (of the prior claim)," the same term as v.23 but here inverted — for walled city houses, permanent sale IS permitted; the Jubilee exception; la-qoneh oto — "for the one who acquired it"; le-dorotav — "throughout his generations"]; it shall not go out in the jubilee [lo tetse be-yovel — לֹא תֵצֵא בַּיֹּבֵל; the key exception: walled-city houses are explicitly excluded from Jubilee reversion; they are treated as movable property rather than ancestral land; the rationale may be that such houses are not part of the tribal land-allotment system, or that urban commerce requires stable property rights].

**25:31** But houses of villages that have no wall surrounding them [u-battei ha-chatserim asher ein lahem chomah saviv — וּבָתֵּי הַחֲצֵרִים אֲשֶׁר אֵין-לָהֶם חֹמָה סָבִיב; chatserim — "villages/unwalled settlements," from chatser, "enclosure/court/settlement"; ein lahem chomah saviv — "there is no wall surrounding them"; the structural criterion separating this category from v.29–30] shall be reckoned [al sadeh ha-aretz yechashev — עַל-שְׂדֵה הָאָרֶץ יֵחָשֵׁב; yechashev — the Niphal imperfect of ch-sh-v, "to be reckoned/classified/regarded as"; al sadeh ha-aretz — "as/upon the field of the land/as agricultural land"; village houses are legally assimilated to agricultural land rather than to walled-city real estate]: redemption shall apply to them [ge'ulah tihyeh lo — גְּאֻלָּה תִּהְיֶה-לּוֹ], and in the jubilee they shall go out [u-va-yovel yetse — וּבַיֹּבֵל יֵצֵא; the reversion applies to village houses as to agricultural land — Jubilee release is universal except for walled-city houses].

**25:32** As for the cities of the Levites [ve-arei ha-Leviyyim — וְעָרֵי הַלְוִיִּם; the Levites receive no tribal land allotment (Num 18:20–24; Josh 13:14) — their "inheritance" is YHWH; instead they receive designated cities within the tribal territories (Num 35); this legislation protects their unique property rights], the houses of the cities of their holding [battei arei achuzatam — בָּתֵּי עָרֵי אֲחֻזָּתָם; their cities are their achuzah — their holding/possession in lieu of agricultural land] — the Levites have a perpetual right of redemption [ge'ulat olam tihyeh la-Leviyyim — גְּאֻלַּת עוֹלָם תִּהְיֶה לַלְוִיִּם; ge'ulat olam — "a redemption of eternity/perpetual right of redemption"; unlike other Israelites whose redemption window for walled-city houses closes after one year (v.29), Levites may redeem their city houses at any time — the temporal limit of v.29 does not apply to them].

**25:33** And if someone redeems from the Levites [va-asher yig'al min-ha-Leviyyim — וַאֲשֶׁר יִגְאַל מִן-הַלְוִיִּם; the case of a Levite-sold property being redeemed, or alternatively — as some read the syntax — the case of someone buying from a Levite; the text is syntactically complex], then the sale of a house [ve-yatsa mimkar bayit — וְיָצָא מִמְכַּר בַּיִת; yatsa — "shall go out/shall be released"; mimkar bayit — "the sale/purchased house"; the house sold reverts] and the city of his holding [ve-ir achuzato — וְעִיר אֲחֻזָּתוֹ; "and the city of his holding" — the Levitical city is treated as the Levite's patrimony] shall go out in the jubilee [ba-yovel — בַּיֹּבֵל; the Jubilee provision applies to Levitical property in their cities]; for the houses of the cities of the Levites [ki battei arei ha-Leviyyim — כִּי בָּתֵּי עָרֵי הַלְוִיִּם; the explanatory ki] are their holding [hi achuzatam — הִוא אֲחֻזָּתָם] among the sons of Yisra'el [be-tokh benei Yisra'el — בְּתוֹךְ בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל; the Levites lack tribal land but their cities function as their achuzah — the same legal category that cannot be permanently alienated for any Israelite tribe; Jubilee therefore protects Levitical cities as it protects all other tribal holdings].

**25:34** But the field of the open land of their cities [u-sdeh migrash arehhem — וּשְׂדֵה מִגְרַשׁ עָרֵיהֶם; migrash — "open land/pastureland/commons surrounding a city" (cf. Num 35:2–5), from g-r-sh, "to drive out/expel/clear"; the open pasture land around the Levitical cities] shall not be sold [lo yimmakher — לֹא יִמָּכֵר; Niphal imperfect — "shall not be sold/shall not be capable of sale"]; for it is a perpetual holding for them [ki achuzat olam hu lahem — כִּי-אֲחֻזַּת עוֹלָם הִוא לָהֶם; achuzat olam — "a perpetual/everlasting holding/possession"; the open commons of Levitical cities are doubly protected — they cannot be sold at all, under any circumstances; the land of Israel's teachers and priests is inalienable in the most absolute sense].

---

**25:35** And if your brother becomes poor [ve-ki yamukh achikhah — וְכִי-יָמוּךְ אָחִיךָ; the third use of yamukh — "sinks/becomes poor" — in the chapter (cf. vv.25, 39, 47); the fraternal obligation cycle begins again at a more personal level: not land but the person of the impoverished brother] and his hand falters beside you [u-matah yado immakh — וּמָטָה יָדוֹ עִמָּךְ; matah yado — "his hand totters/fails/tips," from n-t-h, "to extend/incline/bend/totter"; the hand as the symbol of strength and capacity (cf. v.26); when the hand "fails" economic self-sufficiency collapses; immakh — "beside you/with you" — the impoverished brother is in your immediate community, not a stranger], then you shall hold him fast [ve-hechezaqta bo — וְהֶחֱזַקְתָּ-בּוֹ; hechezaqta — Hifil perfect of ch-z-q, "to strengthen/hold firm/support/seize"; the command is to "grip/hold firm" the failing brother — an economic intervention of practical support before he sinks further] — whether a sojourner or a settler [ger ve-toshav — גֵּר וְתוֹשָׁב; extending the fraternal support even to the resident alien; the ger ve-toshav receives the same care as the native brother; contrast this with vv.44–46 where slaves may be taken from foreign nations but not from fellow Israelites] — and he shall live beside you [ve-chai immakh — וָחַי עִמָּךְ; "and let him live with you" — the goal of all the interventions in this passage is chai — life; the fraternal obligation is ultimately about preserving life itself].

**25:36** Do not take from him interest or profit [al-tiqqach me-itto neshekh ve-tarbit — אַל-תִּקַּח מֵאִתּוֹ נֶשֶׁךְ וְתַרְבִּית; al — the prohibitory negative; tiqqach — Qal jussive of l-q-ch, "to take"; neshekh — "bite/interest," from n-sh-kh, "to bite"; the metaphor of interest as a "bite" taken from the principal — the bite paid at the beginning of a loan by receiving less than the face value; tarbit — "increase/profit," from r-b-h, "to multiply/increase"; the growth paid at the end — more returned than was lent; whether neshekh and tarbit refer to the same phenomenon at two stages of the transaction or to two distinct types of interest is debated; the intent is comprehensive prohibition of any financial gain from loans to poor brothers], and you shall fear your God [ve-yare'ta me-Elohekha — וְיָרֵאתָ מֵאֱלֹהֶיךָ; the motive clause as in v.17 — what one human cannot detect, YHWH observes; the prohibition on interest is grounded in fear of God, not in economic theory alone], and your brother shall live beside you [ve-chai achikhah immakh — וְחַי אָחִיךָ עִמָּךְ; the fraternal formula — achikhah, "your brother" — repeated; the goal is his living, his continued life and dignity in community].

**25:37** Your money you shall not give him at interest [et-kaspekha lo titten lo be-neshekh — אֶת-כַּסְפְּךָ לֹא-תִתֵּן-לוֹ בְּנֶשֶׁךְ; specifying the first type — monetary loans; kaspekha — "your silver/your money"; be-neshekh — "in interest/at a bite"], and for profit you shall not give your food [u-ve-tarbit lo titten okhlekha — וּבְמַרְבִּית לֹא-תִתֵּן אָכְלֶךָ; u-ve-tarbit — "and at profit/increase"; okhlekha — "your food/your provision"; food loans to the poor must be made at face value, not at a profit; the poor man should receive exactly what was agreed, no more demanded in return].

**25:38** I am the LORD your God [ani YHWH Eloheikem — אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם], who brought you out from the land of Egypt [asher hotsethi etkhem me-eretz Mitsrayim — אֲשֶׁר-הוֹצֵאתִי אֶתְכֶם מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם; the Exodus formula as the grounding of the interest prohibition; the rescue from Egyptian slavery is the root of the entire ethic; YHWH redeemed Israel from economic and bodily bondage; therefore Israel must not impose equivalent bondage on fellow Israelites], to give to you the land of Canaan [la-tet lakhem et-eretz Kena'an — לָתֵת לָכֶם אֶת-אֶרֶץ כְּנַעַן], to be God to you [lihyot lakhem le-Elohim — לִהְיוֹת לָכֶם לֵאלֹהִים; the covenant formula in its most compressed form: the gift of land and the gift of relationship — "to be your God" — are given in the same breath; the laws of the jubilee chapter are not arbitrary social policy but the outworking of the covenant relationship].

---

**25:39** And if your brother becomes poor beside you [ve-ki yamukh achikhah immakh — וְכִי-יָמוּךְ אָחִיךָ עִמָּךְ; the fourth use of yamukh; the progression through the chapter has moved from land (vv.25–34) through personal debt (vv.35–38) to bodily enslavement; the economic crisis is now total — the poor brother has nothing left but himself] and sells himself to you [u-mekhar lakh — וּמָכַר לָךְ; the reflexive use of m-k-r — "he sells himself to you"; the ultimate act of desperation: when land is gone and debt cannot be managed, the poor person sells their own labor and person as an indentured servant], you shall not make him serve as a slave [lo ta'avod bo avodat aved — לֹא-תַעֲבֹד בּוֹ עֲבֹדַת עָבֶד; ta'avod — Hifil imperfect of '-b-d, "to cause to work/make labor/impose work"; avodat aved — "the work/service of a slave" — the brutal, coercive service of the permanent bondsman; the prohibition is emphatic: even though the brother has sold himself, he may not be treated as a slave].

**25:40** Like a hired worker, like a settler he shall be with you [ke-sakhir ke-toshav yihyeh immakh — כְּשָׂכִיר כְּתוֹשָׁב יִהְיֶה עִמָּךְ; sakhir — "hired laborer," from s-k-r, "to hire for wages"; toshav — "settler/sojourner" (as in vv.6, 23, 35); the indentured Israelite is to be treated as a free employee, not as property; ke (the preposition "as/like") before each term — comparison, not equation]; until the year of jubilee he shall serve with you [ad shenat ha-yovel ya'avod immakh — עַד שְׁנַת הַיֹּבֵל יַעֲבֹד עִמָּךְ; the maximum term of service is bounded by Jubilee — the system cannot allow permanent slavery of Israelites because Jubilee always comes].

**25:41** And he shall go out from you [ve-yatsa me-immakh — וְיָצָא מֵעִמָּךְ; yatsa — "he shall go out/depart/be released" — the Exodus verb; every indentured Israelite "goes out" from slavery as Israel went out from Egypt; the verbal echo is unmistakable] — he and his sons with him [hu u-vanav immo — הוּא וּבָנָיו עִמּוֹ; the family unit is preserved; the children born during the period of service go free with the father] — and he shall return to his family [ve-shav el-mishpachto — וְשָׁב אֶל-מִשְׁפַּחְתּוֹ; the return to family, as in v.10; Jubilee is restoration of persons as well as land], and to the holding of his fathers he shall return [ve-el achuzat avotav yashuv — וְאֶל-אֲחֻזַּת אֲבֹתָיו יָשׁוּב; the two restorations are inseparable: land and family belong together; the indentured person is not merely freed — he is reconstituted as a member of a family with a specific place on the land].

**25:42** For they are my servants [ki avadai hem — כִּי-עֲבָדַי הֵם; the theological grounding: Israelites cannot be permanently enslaved because they already belong to YHWH as his servants/slaves; the logic is exclusive ownership — one cannot be YHWH's slave and a human master's slave simultaneously] whom I brought out from the land of Egypt [asher hotsethi otam me-eretz Mitsrayim — אֲשֶׁר-הוֹצֵאתִי אֹתָם מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם; the Exodus is again the basis — YHWH's act of redemption established his ownership-relationship with Israel; to re-enslave an Israelite is to undo the Exodus and challenge YHWH's redemptive claim]; they shall not be sold as a slave is sold [lo yimmakeru mimkerat aved — לֹא יִמָּכְרוּ מִמְכֶּרֶת עָבֶד; lo yimmakeru — Niphal imperfect of m-k-r — "they shall not be sold"; mimkeret aved — "in the selling of a slave/as a slave is sold"; the prohibition is not on the sale itself (which is permitted under economic duress) but on the manner of the sale — as if selling a slave-commodity rather than a person with dignity and rights].

**25:43** You shall not rule over him ruthlessly [lo tirdeh vo be-farekh — לֹא-תִרְדֶּה בוֹ בְּפָרֶךְ; lo tirdeh — Qal imperfect of r-d-h, "to rule/have dominion over" (used of God's dominion over creation in Gen 1:28; here of human authority over human); be-farekh — "with crushing labor/with ruthlessness," an unusual word appearing only in Exod 1:13–14 (the description of the Egyptians' brutal treatment of the Israelite slaves in Egypt) and here; the echo is deliberate — the treatment that Israel suffered in Egypt must never be reproduced by Israelites toward Israelites; farekh may relate to an Aramaic root meaning "hard/crushing"], but you shall fear your God [ve-yare'ta me-Elohekha — וְיָרֵאתָ מֵאֱלֹהֶיךָ; the third use of this motive clause in the chapter (cf. vv.17, 36); fear of God is the deterrent against the abuse of power over vulnerable persons whom the law cannot easily protect; the master knows the condition of his servants; only YHWH sees whether the service is with crushing labor or with dignity].

**25:44** And your male slave and your female slave [va-avdekha va-amatekha — וַעַבְדְּךָ וַאֲמָתְךָ; the categories now shift: the preceding legislation protects Israelites from Israelite enslavement; vv.44–46 address a different legal category] that you shall have [asher yihyu lakh — אֲשֶׁר יִהְיוּ-לָךְ] — from the nations that surround you [me-et ha-goyim asher sevivoteikhem — מֵאֵת הַגּוֹיִם אֲשֶׁר סְבִיבֹתֵיכֶם; goyim — "nations/peoples/gentiles"; sevivoteikem — "surrounding you"], from them you may acquire [me-hem tiqnu — מֵהֶם תִּקְנוּ; tiqnu — Qal imperfect of q-n-h, "to acquire/buy/own"] male slave and female slave [aved va-amah — עֶבֶד וְאָמָה; the Torah permits foreign enslavement within the structures of ancient Near Eastern society while protecting Israelites from it; the distinction is explicitly national and covenantal rather than ethnic in the modern sense].

**25:45** And also from the sons of the settlers [ve-gam mi-benei ha-toshavim — וְגַם מִבְּנֵי הַתּוֹשָׁבִים; ha-toshavim — "the settlers," the resident non-Israelites dwelling within Israel's land (as in vv.6, 23, 35, 40); their children may be acquired as slaves] that sojourn among you [ha-garim imakhem — הַגָּרִים עִמָּכֶם; the garim — the sojourners], from them you may acquire [me-hem tiqnu — מֵהֶם תִּקְנוּ] and from their families that are with you [u-mi-mishpechotam asher imakhem — וּמִמִּשְׁפְּחֹתָם אֲשֶׁר עִמָּכֶם], whom they have fathered in your land [asher holidu be-artsekhem — אֲשֶׁר-הוֹלִידוּ בְּאַרְצְכֶם; holidu — Hifil perfect of y-l-d, "to cause to be born/to beget/to father"], and they shall be your property [ve-hayu lakhem la-achuzah — וְהָיוּ לָכֶם לַאֲחֻזָּה; la-achuzah — "as a holding/possession," the same word used throughout the chapter for ancestral land; the use of achuzah for slaves is deliberate — they become part of the household's inherited property].

**25:46** And you shall bequeath them [ve-hithnachaltem otam — וְהִתְנַחַלְתֶּם אוֹתָם; hithnachaltem — Hitpael perfect of n-ch-l, "to cause oneself to inherit/to take as an inheritance/to bequeath"; the Hitpael here has a causative-declarative sense: "you shall bequeath them to one another"; the slaves of non-Israelite origin may be passed on to children as heritable property] to your sons after you [li-vneikem achreikem — לִבְנֵיכֶם אַחֲרֵיכֶם] to inherit as a holding [la-reshet achuzah — לָרֶשֶׁת אֲחֻזָּה]; you may put them to work [ta'avidu vahem — תַּעֲבִדוּ בָהֶם; ta'avidu — Hifil imperfect of '-b-d, "to cause to work/impose labor upon"; the Hifil form used in v.39 with the prohibition for Israelites is here permitted for foreign slaves] forever [le-olam — לְעוֹלָם]; but over your brothers the sons of Yisra'el [u-ve-acheikem benei Yisra'el — וּבַאֲחֵיכֶם בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל], a man over his brother [ish be-achiv — אִישׁ בְּאָחִיו; the intensification of fraternal vocabulary is deliberate — "a man over his brother"] — you shall not rule over him ruthlessly [lo tirdeh vo be-farekh — לֹא-תִרְדֶּה-בוֹ בְּפָרֶךְ; the prohibition of v.43 is now restated at the section's close, forming a frame; the repetition of be-farekh — "with crushing labor" — the Egyptian word — makes the Exodus echo explicit: Israelites must never become Egypt to their brothers].

---

**25:47** And if the hand of a sojourner or settler with you attains enough [ve-ki tasig yad ger ve-toshav immakh — וְכִי תַשִּׂיג יַד-גֵּר וְתוֹשָׁב עִמָּךְ; tasig yad — "the hand reaches/attains," the same idiom as v.26; the reversal of economic fortune — the alien prospers while the Israelite falls], and your brother becomes poor beside him [ve-amukh achikhah immo — וְאָמוֹךְ אָחִיךָ עִמּוֹ; amokh — from m-w-kh, "to become poor," as throughout; the social inversion is striking: the ger and toshav, who in much of the ancient world held the lowest social status, here have the financial power to "own" an Israelite] and he sells himself to the sojourner or settler with you [u-mekhar le-ger toshav immakh — וּמָכַר לְגֵר תּוֹשָׁב עִמָּךְ], or to a branch of a foreign family [o le-eker mishpachat ger — אוֹ לְעֵקֶר מִשְׁפַּחַת גֵּר; eker — "root/stock/branch/member," from '-q-r, "to uproot/pull out" — possibly referring to someone of partial or naturalized foreign stock; mishpachat ger — "a foreign family/a family of the ger"; the two cases cover a range of non-Israelite ownership]:

**25:48** After he is sold [achar nimkar — אַחֲרֵי הִמָּכֵר; nimkar — Niphal perfect of m-k-r, "after having been sold/after he was sold"] redemption shall remain for him [ge'ulah tihyeh lo — גְּאֻלָּה תִהְיֶה-לּוֹ; the right of redemption cannot be extinguished even when an Israelite is owned by a foreigner]; one of his brothers may redeem him [echad me-echav yig'alenu — אֶחָד מֵאֶחָיו יִגְאָלֶנּוּ; echad me-echav — "one from among his brothers" — any kinsman may act as go'el; yig'alenu — Qal imperfect of g-'-l with 3ms suffix — "shall redeem him"].

**25:49** Or his uncle or his uncle's son may redeem him [o-dodo o-ven-dodo yig'alenu — אוֹ-דֹדוֹ אוֹ-בֶן-דֹדוֹ יִגְאָלֶנּוּ; dodo — "his uncle" (specifically his father's brother, from d-w-d); ven-dodo — "his uncle's son," his first cousin; the kinship chain for go'el obligations is specified in descending proximity: brothers first, then paternal uncle, then paternal first cousin], or any close relative from his family [o mi-she'er besaro mi-mishpachto — אוֹ-מִשְּׁאֵר בְּשָׂרוֹ מִמִּשְׁפַּחְתּוֹ; she'er besaro — "close of his flesh/near kinsman," she'er from sh-'-r, "to remain" or used as a term for near kinsman, besaro — "his flesh"; the category is deliberately open-ended to include any sufficiently close relative] may redeem him [yig'alenu — יִגְאָלֶנּוּ]; and if he himself attains enough [o hassigah yado — אוֹ הִשִּׂיגָה יָדוֹ; self-redemption remains possible as in v.26] he may redeem himself [ve-nigal — וְנִגְאָל; the Niphal perfect — "and he shall be redeemed," allowing for the passive sense of self-redemption or of being redeemed by his own means].

**25:50** And he shall calculate with his acquirer [ve-chishav im-qoneihu — וְחִשַּׁב עִם-קֹנֵהוּ; the same verb chishav as in v.27 — formal accounting; qoneihu — "his acquirer/his owner"; the calculation is bilateral and fair, not unilaterally dictated by the master] from the year of his being sold to him [mi-shenat himmakreo lo — מִשְּׁנַת הִמָּכְרוֹ לוֹ; "from the year of his being sold to him"] until the year of jubilee [ad shenat ha-yovel — עַד שְׁנַת הַיֹּבֵל], and the price of his selling shall be in accordance with the number of years [ve-hayah kesef mimkaro be-mispar shanim — וְהָיָה כֶּסֶף מִמְכָּרוֹ בְּמִסְפַּר שָׁנִים; the redemption price is calculated by how many years of service remain — the same principle as land redemption in vv.15–16]; like the days of a hired worker it shall be with him [ki-yemei sakhir yihyeh immo — כִּימֵי שָׂכִיר יִהְיֶה עִמּוֹ; sakhir — the hired day-laborer, whose service is by definition temporary and terminable; the Israelite indentured to a foreigner is treated as a sakhir — a hired employee — even in foreign ownership].

**25:51** If yet many years remain [im od rabboth ba-shanim — אִם עוֹד רַבּוֹת בַּשָּׁנִים; rabboth — "many/great," feminine plural agreeing with shanim], in proportion to them he shall return his redemption-payment [le-feihen yashiv ge'ulato — לְפִיהֶן יָשִׁיב גְּאֻלָּתוֹ; le-feihen — "according to their mouths/in proportion to them"; yashiv — Hifil imperfect of sh-w-v, "to return/give back"] from the silver of his purchase [mi-kesef miknato — מִכֶּסֶף מִקְנָתוֹ; "from the silver of his acquisition/purchase price"].

**25:52** And if few years remain until the year of jubilee [ve-im me'at nish'ar ba-shanim ad shenat ha-yovel — וְאִם-מְעַט נִשְׁאַר בַּשָּׁנִים עַד-שְׁנַת הַיֹּבֵל; nish'ar — Niphal perfect of sh-'-r, "to remain/be left"; the closer to Jubilee, the smaller the redemption payment — fewer years of labor remain to compensate], he shall calculate accordingly [ve-chishav lo ke-fi shanov — וְחִשַּׁב-לוֹ כְּפִי שָׁנָיו; ke-fi shanov — "according to his years/in proportion to his years"]; in proportion to his years he shall return his redemption [yashiv et-ge'ulato — יָשִׁיב אֶת-גְּאֻלָּתוֹ].

**25:53** Like a hired worker year by year he shall be with him [ki-sakhir shanah be-shanah yihyeh immo — כִּשְׂכִיר שָׁנָה בְּשָׁנָה יִהְיֶה עִמּוֹ; the sakhir-framework is restated; shanah be-shanah — "year in year/year by year" — the hired worker's annual contract; even in foreign ownership the Israelite's labor is reckoned on fair, annual hired-worker terms]; he shall not rule over him ruthlessly in your sight [lo yirdenu be-farekh le-einekha — לֹא יִרְדֶּנּוּ בְּפָרֶךְ לְעֵינֶיךָ; lo yirdenu — "he shall not rule him crushingly"; be-farekh — the Egyptian word again; le-einekha — "before your eyes/in your sight"; the community bears witness to the treatment of the Israelite held by a foreigner — it is not a private matter; the community's observation creates accountability; the Israelite community must ensure that farekh does not happen, even in a foreigner's household within their midst].

**25:54** And if he is not redeemed by these means [ve-im lo yiggael be-elleh — וְאִם לֹא יִגָּאֵל בְּאֵלֶּה; be-elleh — "by these/through these means" — the redemption mechanisms described in vv.48–53], then he shall go out in the year of jubilee [ve-yatsa bi-shenat ha-yovel — וְיָצָא בִּשְׁנַת הַיֹּבֵל; yatsa — the Exodus verb; even the Israelite sold to a foreigner who cannot be redeemed by any kinsman and cannot self-redeem is not permanently enslaved — Jubilee releases him absolutely; the Jubilee is the ultimate backstop of the entire system] — he and his sons with him [hu u-vanav immo — הוּא וּבָנָיו עִמּוֹ; as in v.41, family unity is preserved; the sons go free with the father].

**25:55** For to me [ki-li — כִּי-לִי; the same construction as ki-li ha-aretz in v.23 — "for mine"; the final verse echoes the chapter's theological center] the sons of Yisra'el are servants [benei Yisra'el avadim — בְּנֵי-יִשְׂרָאֵל עֲבָדִים; they are YHWH's servants, belonging exclusively to him; as the land cannot be permanently alienated because it belongs to YHWH (v.23), so the sons of Yisra'el cannot be permanently enslaved because they belong to YHWH] — they are my servants [avadai hem — עֲבָדַי הֵם; the direct declaration, repeating ki-avadai hem from v.42; the chapter closes by returning to its theological foundation: the reason no Israelite can be permanently enslaved is that YHWH is their ultimate and exclusive master] whom I brought out from the land of Egypt [asher hotsethi otam me-eretz Mitsrayim — אֲשֶׁר הוֹצֵאתִי אוֹתָם מֵאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם; the Exodus formula, the third iteration in this chapter (cf. vv.38, 42) — the Exodus is the inexhaustible theological warrant for the Jubilee's social vision]: I am the LORD your God [ani YHWH Eloheikem — אֲנִי יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם; the divine self-identification formula that ends the entire chapter — the same formula that opens the Ten Words (Exod 20:2) and punctuates the Holiness Code throughout; it is declaration, not argument; the laws of Leviticus 25 rest ultimately not on social utility or economic theory but on who YHWH is and what YHWH has done].

---

## Translator's Notes

**On the structure of Leviticus 25:** The chapter divides into six sections: (1) the shemittah — sabbatical year for the land (vv.1–7); (2) the yovel — jubilee year proclamation (vv.8–17); (3) the divine provision promise (vv.18–22); (4) the theological foundation and land-redemption law (vv.23–34); (5) the care for the impoverished brother (vv.35–46); and (6) the redemption of an Israelite from a foreigner (vv.47–55). The progression is not random — it moves from the land to the person, from cosmic (the land's sabbath) to personal (the enslaved individual), with the Exodus formula appearing as the grounding refrain throughout.

**On yovel (vv.10–13, 28, 31, etc.):** Preserved as "jubilee" throughout, this being an established English rendering derived from the Latin iubilaeus, itself from the Hebrew yovel. The Hebrew term likely means "ram/ram's horn" (the instrument sounded to proclaim the year) or possibly is connected to the root y-b-l, "to carry/lead." The translation "jubilee" carries sufficient tradition weight and avoids the awkward literalism of "ram's-horn year."

**On deror (v.10):** Translated "liberty" following the weight of the term's usage in ancient Near Eastern law (Akkadian andurarum) and its resonance in the English-language tradition (Liberty Bell inscription). A more literal rendering might be "release," but "liberty" captures the proclamatory, royal character of the declaration — it is not merely private release but public announcement.

**On ge'ulah / go'el (vv.24–33, 47–55):** "Redemption" and "redeemer/kinsman-redeemer" following EMET conventions. The root g-'-l is one of the most theologically freighted in the Hebrew Bible: Boaz is the go'el in Ruth; YHWH is Israel's Go'el in Deutero-Isaiah; the connection between kinsman-redeemer and divine redeemer is not accidental to the theology of the chapter.

**On be-farekh (vv.43, 46, 53):** Transliterated in annotation, translated "ruthlessly/with crushing labor." The word appears only in Exod 1:13–14 and these three verses in Lev 25. The Exodus-echo is the point: the way the Egyptians treated Israel (be-farekh) is the template for what Israelites must never do to one another. The word functions as a counter-Exodus marker — if Israel acts be-farekh, it has become Egypt.

**On the double use of ki-li (vv.23 and 55):** The chapter's inclusio is formed by the two ki-li declarations: ki-li ha-aretz (v.23) — "for the land is mine" — and ki-li benei Yisra'el avadim (v.55) — "for the sons of Yisra'el are servants to me." Both land and persons belong exclusively to YHWH. All the chapter's legislation is the practical consequence of these two ownership claims.

**On neshekh ve-tarbit (vv.36–37):** "Interest and profit" — the two terms are rendered distinctly following the traditional understanding: neshekh ("bite") as interest charged at the front end of a loan (the loan disbursed at less than face value), tarbit as increase charged at the back end (more returned than was given). Some scholars argue they are synonyms; the text preserves the distinction and so does this translation.

**On the structure of the slavery legislation (vv.39–55):** The passage makes a sharp distinction between Israelites-enslaved-by-Israelites (vv.39–43), where the institution is structurally abolished through the sakhir-framework and Jubilee release; and non-Israelites acquired as slaves (vv.44–46), where permanent enslavement is permitted. A third category, Israelites-enslaved-by-foreigners (vv.47–55), is treated with special concern — the right of redemption by kinsman continues, and Jubilee releases them if unredeemed. The chapter does not abolish slavery as an institution but systematically dismantles it for members of the covenant community and strictly limits its most brutal expressions even for non-Israelites in the Israelite household.
